html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Six — Part One

The Book of Chanokh

CHANOKH

The Book of the Watchers — DSS Attested

Chapters 1 through 36

The Watchers Descended on Mount Hermon — Chanokh Interceded Before YHWH — The Cosmic Journeys — The Judgement of the Fallen — All the Glory of the Righteous Shall Be Great

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — 4QEna (4Q201) · 4QEnb (4Q202) · 4QEnc (4Q204) — Aramaic Primary

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — Primary Witness

YHWH and The Creator only — DSS Attested Sections Only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

THE BOOK OF THE WATCHERS — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 36

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 1 The Opening Vision — The Coming of YHWH

1 The words of the blessing of Chanokh, with which he blessed the elect and righteous, who will be living in the day of tribulation, when all the wicked and godless are to be removed.

2 And he took up his parable and said: Chanokh, a righteous man whose eyes were opened by The Creator, who had the vision of the Holy One and of heaven, which he showed me — from the words of the watchers and holy ones I heard everything, and as I heard everything from them, I also understood what I saw — but not for this generation, but for a remote one which is to come.

3 Concerning the elect I spoke, and took up my parable concerning them: The Holy Great One will come forth from his dwelling,

4 And the eternal Creator will tread upon the earth, even upon Mount Sinai, and appear in the strength of his might from the heaven of heavens.

5 And all shall be smitten with fear, and the Watchers shall quake, and great fear and trembling shall seize them unto the ends of the earth.

6 And the high mountains shall be shaken, and the high hills shall be made low, and shall melt like wax before the flame.

7 And the earth shall be wholly rent in sunder, and all that is upon the earth shall perish, and there shall be a judgment upon all.

8 But with the righteous he will make peace, and will protect the elect, and mercy shall be upon them. And they shall all belong to The Creator, and they shall be prospered, and they shall all be blessed.

9 And he will help them all, and light shall appear unto them, and he will make peace with them. Behold, he comes with ten thousands of his holy ones to execute judgment upon all, and to destroy all the ungodly, and to convict all flesh of all the works of their ungodliness which they have ungodly committed, and of all the hard things which ungodly sinners have spoken against him.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEna (4Q201) preserves portions of the opening chapters of Chanokh in Aramaic. The Book of the Watchers — Chapters 1-36 — is the most extensively attested portion of Chanokh at Qumran, found across six manuscripts from Cave 4. The Aramaic text of Qumran is the oldest surviving manuscript of Chanokh in any language, predating the Ge'ez manuscripts by approximately a thousand years. The Ge'ez and Aramaic texts are in substantial agreement throughout the Book of the Watchers.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves all 108 chapters of Chanokh as canonical scripture within the Beta Israel Ethiopian tradition. The Ge'ez text is the only complete surviving version of the full book. The Book of the Watchers in Ge'ez aligns closely with the Qumran Aramaic fragments wherever they overlap.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] Chanokh is not present in the Masoretic canon. The Masoretic tradition does not preserve this text. The DSS Aramaic fragments and the Orit Ge'ez are the exclusive textual authorities for this volume.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1:9 — he comes with ten thousands of his holy ones to execute judgment upon all. This verse was later cited in extra-canonical writings as a quotation from Chanokh son of Yered the seventh from Adam. The citation confirms that the Book of Chanokh was known and treated as authoritative scripture in the Second Temple period community. The DSS manuscripts physically confirm the book's presence at Qumran as a text studied and preserved alongside the Torah. The book's exclusion from the Masoretic canon is a post-Second Temple Rabbinic decision, not a reflection of its standing in the ancient Yisra'ELi community.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 2 The Observation of Nature — The Faithfulness of Creation

1 Observe everything that takes place in the heaven, how they do not change their orbits, and the luminaries which are in the heaven, how they all rise and set in order each in its season, and transgress not their appointed order.

2 Behold the earth, and give heed to the things which take place upon it from first to last, how steadfast they are, how none of the things upon earth change, but all the works of The Creator appear to you.

3 Behold the summer and the winter, how the whole earth is filled with water, and clouds and dew and rain lie upon it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEna preserves portions of Chapter 2 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Chapters 2-5 form a sustained meditation on the obedience of the natural creation in contrast to the disobedience of humanity. Every element of creation — stars, sun, moon, trees, seasons — keeps its appointed order. Only the human beings and the Watchers have transgressed their ordained boundaries. The argument of the Book of the Watchers begins with creation's faithfulness as the standard against which the rebellion of the Watchers is measured.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 3 The Trees and Their Seasons

1 Observe and see how all the trees wither and all their leaves fall off, except fourteen trees, which do not lose their foliage but retain the old foliage from two to three years until the new comes.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEna preserves a portion of Chapter 3.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The fourteen trees that retain their foliage are observed as a natural sign of continuity within the cycles of change. The natural world demonstrates both seasonal transformation and enduring persistence. Creation's patterns are not chaotic — they are appointed and ordered. Even the apparent exceptions to seasonal loss are part of the created order.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 4 Summer and Winter

1 Observe how the days in summer are long, and how in winter the nights have become so long, while the day has become as short as the night, and how all the days are fulfilled which the sun is ordered in its circuit.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEna preserves a portion of Chapter 4 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The astronomical regularity observed in Chapter 4 connects directly to the Astronomical Book of Chanokh — Chapters 72-82 — which provides an extensive technical treatment of the solar and lunar calendars. The opening chapters establish that natural creation's obedience is the foundation of the moral argument that follows.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 5 The Accusation Against the Sinners — The Promise to the Righteous

1 Observe how the trees cover themselves with green leaves and bear fruit — understand and know with regard to all his works, and recognize how he that lives forever made them so.

2 And all his works go on thus from year to year forever, and all the tasks which they accomplish for him, and their tasks change not, but according as The Creator has ordained so is it done.

3 And behold how the sea and the rivers in like manner accomplish and change not their tasks from his commandments.

4 But you have not been steadfast, nor done the commandments of the YHWH, but you have turned away and spoken proud and hard words with your impure mouths against his greatness. O you hard-hearted, you shall find no peace.

5 Therefore shall you execrate your days, and the years of your life shall perish, and the years of your destruction shall be multiplied in eternal execration, and you shall find no mercy.

6 In those days you shall make your names an eternal execration unto all the righteous, and by you shall all who curse, curse, and all the sinners and godless shall imprecate by you.

7 And for the elect there shall be light and joy and peace, and they shall inherit the earth. And then there shall be bestowed upon the elect wisdom, and they shall all live and never again sin, either through ungodliness or through pride. But they who are wise shall be humble.

8 And they shall not again transgress, nor shall they sin all the days of their life, nor shall they die of the divine anger or wrath, but they shall complete the number of the days of their life. And their lives shall be increased in peace, and the years of their joy shall be multiplied, in eternal gladness and peace, all the days of their life.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEna preserves portions of Chapter 5 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5:7 — for the elect there shall be light and joy and peace, and they shall inherit the earth. The opening section of Chanokh closes with the promise to the elect — those who remain faithful within the covenant. The language of inheriting the earth and the contrast between the fate of the wicked and the fate of the righteous frames the entire book. The Watchers story that follows in Chapters 6-11 is the foundational explanation for why the earth is in its corrupted condition — and why the judgment announced in Chapter 1 is necessary.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 6 The Watchers Descend — The Oath on Mount Hermon

1 And it came to pass when the children of men had multiplied that in those days were born unto them beautiful and comely daughters.

2 And the angels, the children of heaven, saw and lusted after them, and said to one another: Come, let us choose wives for ourselves from among the children of men and beget us children.

3 And Shemikhazah, who was their leader, said unto them: I fear that you will not indeed agree to do this deed, and I alone shall have to pay the penalty of a great sin.

4 And they all answered him and said: Let us all swear an oath, and all bind ourselves by mutual imprecations not to abandon this plan but to do this thing.

5 Then they all swore together and bound themselves by mutual imprecations upon it.

6 And they were in all two hundred, who descended in the days of Yered on the summit of Mount Hermon, and they called it Mount Hermon, because they had sworn and bound themselves by mutual imprecations upon it.

7 And these are the names of their leaders: Shemikhazah, their leader, Arakiba, Ra'ameel, Kokabi'el, Tami'el, Rami'el, Dani'el, Ezek'el, Baraqyal, Asael, Armoros, Batare'el, Anane'el, Zaqiel, Samsapeel, Satare'el, Turel, Yomyael, Sarizyal.

8 These are their chiefs of tens.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEna (4Q201) and 4QEnb (4Q202) preserve substantial portions of Chapter 6 in Aramaic. The names of the Watcher leaders are among the most carefully attested details in the DSS manuscripts. The descent of two hundred Watchers on Mount Hermon is a central historical claim of the Book of the Watchers. Mount Hermon — located in the northern region of the land — is geographically identified in the ancient text as the specific site of the descent.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6:1-2 — the connection to Bereshit 6:1-4 is direct. The sons of The Creator who saw the daughters of men and took them as wives in Bereshit are identified in Chanokh as the Watchers — a specific class of heavenly beings assigned to watch over and instruct humanity. Their transgression is presented as a deliberate, planned, and collectively sworn act — not an inadvertent boundary crossing. The oath on Mount Hermon binds them to their transgression before it is committed. The naming of their two hundred leaders establishes individual accountability for the act.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 7 The Nephilim — The Teaching of Forbidden Knowledge

1 And all the others together with them took unto themselves wives, and each chose for himself one, and they began to go in unto them and to defile themselves with them, and they taught them charms and enchantments, and the cutting of roots, and made them acquainted with plants.

2 And the women became pregnant, and they bare great giants, whose height was three thousand ells.

3 Who consumed all the acquisitions of men. And when men could no longer sustain them,

4 The giants turned against them and devoured mankind.

5 And they began to sin against birds, and beasts, and reptiles, and fish, and to devour one another's flesh, and drink the blood.

6 Then the earth laid accusation against the lawless ones.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEna and 4QEnb preserve substantial portions of Chapter 7 in Aramaic. The physical dimensions of the Nephilim as preserved in the DSS Aramaic differ somewhat from the Ge'ez — the precise height figures vary between manuscript traditions. Both are preserved here as stated in the Ge'ez text. The core account — Watcher union, giant offspring, devastation of earth and humanity — is consistent across both textual witnesses.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7:6 — the earth laid accusation against the lawless ones. This is one of the most striking phrases in the ancient literature. The earth itself, as a covenant creation, becomes the accuser of those who have violated its order. The same concept appears in Bamidbar 35:33 — blood pollutes the land — and in Bereshit 4:10 — the blood of Hevel cries out from the ground. The earth in the ancient Hebrew understanding is not a passive substrate. It is a witness to and respondent to moral events. The Nephilim's devastation of creation causes creation itself to bring its complaint before YHWH.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 8 The Teachings of the Fallen Watchers — Azazel and the Others

1 And Azazel taught men to make swords, and knives, and shields, and breastplates, and made known to them the metals of the earth and the art of working them, and bracelets, and ornaments, and the use of antimony, and the beautifying of the eyelids, and all kinds of costly stones, and all colouring tinctures.

2 And there arose much godlessness, and they committed fornication, and they were led astray, and became corrupt in all their ways.

3 Shemikhazah taught enchantments and root-cuttings. Armaros taught the resolving of enchantments. Baraqyal taught astrology. Kokabi'el taught the constellations. Ezekeel taught the knowledge of the clouds. Araqiel taught the signs of the earth. Shamsiel taught the signs of the sun. Sariel taught the course of the moon.

4 And as men perished, they cried, and their cry went up to heaven.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEna and 4QEnb preserve portions of Chapter 8 in Aramaic. Azazel's specific role as the teacher of metalwork and weapons manufacture is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts. The list of forbidden knowledge transmitted by each Watcher leader is one of the most detailed passages in the DSS Chanokh fragments.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Chapter 8 is the ancient record's account of the origin of several categories of human knowledge and technology. Weapons, cosmetics, astrology, knowledge of roots and plants — all are attributed to specific Watcher leaders who transmitted them to humanity before the proper time or through channels YHWH had not authorized. The theology is not that these arts are inherently evil but that their transmission outside the proper covenant order corrupted human society. The cry of perishing men in verse 8:4 reaches heaven — triggering the divine response of Chapters 9-10.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 9 The Four Archangels Intercede — The Report to YHWH

1 And then Mikha'el, Uriel, Rafa'el, and Gavri'el looked down from heaven and saw much blood being shed upon the earth, and all lawlessness being wrought upon the earth.

2 And they said one to another: The earth made without inhabitant cries the voice of their crying up to the gates of heaven.

3 And now to you, the holy ones of heaven, the souls of men make their suit, saying: Bring our cause before the Most High.

4 And they said to the Lord of the ages: Lord of lords, Creator of creators, King of kings, and Creator of the ages, the throne of your glory stands unto all the generations of the ages, and your name is holy and glorious, and blessed unto all the ages.

5 You have made all things, and power over all things you have, and all things are naked and open in your sight, and you see all things, and nothing can hide itself from you.

6 You see what Azazel has done, who has taught all unrighteousness on earth and revealed the eternal secrets which were preserved in heaven, which men were striving to learn.

7 And Shemikhazah, to whom you have given authority to bear rule over his associates — and they have gone to the daughters of men upon the earth, and have slept with the women, and have defiled themselves, and revealed to them all kinds of sins.

8 And the women have borne giants, and the whole earth has thereby been filled with blood and unrighteousness.

9 And now, behold, the souls of those who have died are crying and making their suit to the gates of heaven, and their lamentations have ascended, and cannot cease because of the lawless deeds which are wrought on the earth.

10 And you know all things before they come to pass, and you see these things and you do suffer them, and you do not say to us what we are to do to them in regard to these.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnb (4Q202) preserves portions of Chapter 9 in Aramaic. The intercession of the four archangels — Mikha'el, Uriel, Rafa'el, and Gavri'el — is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts. These four are the same archangels named in the broader Second Temple period literature and in the Qumran community's angelology as found in the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice (4QShirShab).

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9:6 — Azazel has revealed the eternal secrets which were preserved in heaven. The specific transgression of the Watchers is the unauthorized disclosure of heavenly knowledge to humanity. The secrets of metallurgy, weaponry, cosmetics, astrology, and the other arts were preserved in heaven — not because they are inherently evil but because their transmission to earth was governed by YHWH's schedule and authorization. The Watchers broke the boundary between heavenly and earthly knowledge outside YHWH's appointed order. The archangels' report to YHWH frames this as the foundational crime from which all subsequent corruption flows.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 10 The Judgment Commanded — Bind Azazel — Destroy the Nephilim — The Flood

1 Then said the Most High, the Holy and Great One spoke, and sent Uriel to the son of Lamekh, and said to him:

2 Go to Noakh and tell him in my name: Hide yourself and reveal to him the end that is approaching, that the whole earth will be destroyed, and a deluge is about to come upon the whole earth, and will destroy all that is on it.

3 And now instruct him that he may escape and his seed may be preserved for all the generations of the world.

4 And again YHWH said to Rafa'el: Bind Azazel hand and foot, and cast him into the darkness, and make an opening in the desert, which is in Dudael, and cast him therein.

5 And place upon him rough and jagged rocks, and cover him with darkness, and let him abide there forever, and cover his face that he may not see light.

6 And on the day of the great judgment he shall be cast into the fire.

7 And heal the earth which the angels have corrupted, and proclaim the healing of the earth, that they may heal the plague, and that all the children of men may not perish through all the secret things that the Watchers have disclosed and have taught their sons.

8 And the whole earth has been corrupted through the works that were taught by Azazel — to him ascribe all sin.

9 And to Gavri'el said YHWH: Proceed against the bastards and the reprobates, and against the children of fornication, and destroy the children of the Watchers from among men. Send them one against the other that they may destroy each other in battle, for length of days shall they not have.

10 And YHWH said unto Mikha'el: Go, bind Shemikhazah and his associates who have united themselves with women so as to have defiled themselves with them in all their uncleanness.

11 And when their sons have slain one another, and they have seen the destruction of their beloved ones, bind them fast for seventy generations in the valleys of the earth, till the day of their judgment and of their consummation, till the judgment that is forever and ever is consummated.

12 In those days they shall be led off to the abyss of fire, and to the torment and the prison in which they shall be confined forever.

13 And then shall the whole earth be tilled in righteousness, and shall all be planted with trees and be full of blessing.

14 And all desirable trees shall be planted on it, and they shall plant vines on it, and the vine which they plant thereon shall yield wine in abundance, and as for all the seed which is sown thereon each measure shall bear a thousand, and each measure of olives shall yield ten presses of oil.

15 And all the children of men shall become righteous, and all nations shall offer adoration and shall praise me, and all shall worship me.

16 And the earth shall be cleansed from all defilement, and from all sin, and from all punishment, and from all torment, and I will never again send a flood upon it from generation to generation and forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnb (4Q202) and 4QEnc (4Q204) preserve substantial portions of Chapter 10 in Aramaic. The binding of Azazel in the desert — Dudael — and the assignment of each archangel to a specific judgment task are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts. Chapter 10 is one of the most extensively attested chapters of Chanokh in the DSS corpus.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10:8 — to Azazel ascribe all sin. This is the theological linchpin of the Azazel theology in the Book of the Watchers. The scapegoat ritual of Vayikra 16 — where a goat is sent to Azazel in the wilderness bearing the sins of the community — connects directly to this declaration. The name Azazel in the Yom Kippur liturgy of Vayikra is not a neutral geographic term. It is the name of the bound Watcher, cast into the desert, covered with rocks, in darkness — and the ritual of sending a sin-bearing goat to that location is the covenant community's enacted declaration that sin belongs with its originator. The Qumran community's extensive interest in both the Yom Kippur liturgy and the Chanokh literature confirms they understood this connection.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 11 The Renewal of the Earth After the Judgment

1 And in those days I will open the store chambers of blessing which are in the heaven, so as to send them down upon the earth over the work and labor of the children of men.

2 And truth and peace shall be associated together throughout all the days of the world and throughout all the generations of men.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnc preserves a portion of Chapter 11.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 11 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The brief Chapter 11 closes the judgment section with the promise of restoration. The store chambers of blessing in heaven will be opened for the earth. Truth and peace will be joined for all the days of the world. The eschatological hope of Chanokh is not merely punitive — the judgment of the Watchers and the Nephilim clears the earth for restoration and blessing.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 12 Chanokh Before the Watchers — The Commission of Chanokh

1 Before these things Chanokh was hidden, and no one of the children of men knew where he was hidden, and where he abode, and what had become of him.

2 And his activities had to do with the Watchers, and his days were with the holy ones.

3 And I, Chanokh, was blessing the Lord of majesty and the King of the ages, and lo, the Watchers called me — Chanokh the scribe — and said to me:

4 Chanokh, you scribe of righteousness, go and declare to the Watchers of the heaven who have left the high heaven, the holy eternal place, and have defiled themselves with women, and have done as the children of earth do, and have taken unto themselves wives — you have wrought great destruction on the earth.

5 And you shall have no peace nor forgiveness of sin. And inasmuch as they delight themselves in their children,

6 The murder of their beloved ones shall they see, and over the destruction of their children shall they lament, and shall make supplication unto eternity, but mercy and peace shall you not attain.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnc (4Q204) preserves portions of Chapter 12 in Aramaic. The identification of Chanokh as the scribe of righteousness — sofer tzedek — is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 12 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12:3 — Chanokh the scribe of righteousness. This title places Chanokh in the role of an intercessor and messenger between the heavenly and earthly realms. He is asked to bring the sentence of YHWH to the Watchers who are already aware of their condemnation. Chanokh functions as a covenant mediator in the reverse direction from Moshe — Moshe brought YHWH's words to the people of earth; Chanokh brings YHWH's judgment to the fallen heavenly beings. The role of scribe is significant: the judgment is written, not merely spoken.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 13 Chanokh Writes the Petition of the Watchers — The Dream Vision

1 And Chanokh went and said: Azazel, you shalt have no peace — a severe sentence has gone forth against you to put you in bonds.

2 And you shall not have toleration nor request granted to you, because of the unrighteousness which you have taught, and because of all the works of godlessness and unrighteousness and sin which you have shown to men.

3 Then I went and spoke to them all together, and they were all afraid, and fear and trembling seized them.

4 And they besought me to draw up a petition for them that they might find forgiveness, and to read their petition in the presence of YHWH of heaven.

5 For from thenceforward they could not speak with him nor lift up their eyes to heaven for shame of their sins for which they had been condemned.

6 Then I wrote out their petition, and the prayer in regard to their spirits and their deeds individually and in regard to their requests that they should have forgiveness and length of days.

7 And I went off and sat down at the waters of Dan, in the land of Dan, to the south of the west of Hermon — I read their petition till I fell asleep.

8 And behold a dream came to me, and visions fell down upon me, and I saw visions of chastisement, and a voice came bidding me to tell it to the sons of heaven, and reprimand them.

9 And when I awoke, I came unto them, and they were all sitting gathered together, weeping in Abelsjail, which is between Lebanon and Seneser, with their faces covered.

10 And I recounted before them all the visions which I had seen in sleep, and I began to speak the words of righteousness, and to reprimand the heavenly Watchers.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnc (4Q204) preserves portions of Chapter 13 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 13 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Watchers cannot approach YHWH directly because of their sin. They cannot lift their eyes to heaven. They ask Chanokh — a human being, a mortal — to intercede for them before YHWH. The reversal is complete: the heavenly beings who overstepped their ordained boundary to come down to earth now need an earthly man to carry their petition upward. Chanokh's unique status — walked with The Creator, was taken — positions him as the one being who can move between the two realms. His petition for the Watchers is refused, as Chapter 14 will demonstrate.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 14 The Throne Vision — Chanokh Before the Great Glory

1 The book of the words of righteousness, and of the reprimand of the eternal Watchers in accordance with the command of the Holy Great One in that vision.

2 I saw in my sleep what I will now say with a tongue of flesh and with the breath of my mouth, which the Great One has given to men to converse therewith and understand with the heart.

3 As he has created and given to man the power of understanding the word of wisdom, so has he created me also and given me the power of reprimanding the Watchers, the children of heaven.

4 And the visions were shown to me thus: Behold, in the vision clouds invited me and a mist summoned me, and the course of the stars and the lightnings sped and hastened me, and the winds in the vision caused me to fly and lifted me upward, and bore me into heaven.

5 And I went in till I drew nigh to a wall which was built of crystals and surrounded by tongues of fire, and it began to affright me.

6 And I went into the tongues of fire and drew nigh to a large house which was built of crystals, and the walls of the house were like a tessellated floor made of crystals, and its groundwork was of crystal.

7 Its ceiling was like the path of the stars and the lightnings, and between them were fiery cherubim, and their heaven was water.

8 And a flaming fire surrounded the walls, and its portals blazed with fire.

9 And I entered into that house, and it was hot as fire and cold as ice — there were no delights of life therein. Fear covered me, and trembling got hold upon me.

10 And I observed and saw therein a lofty throne, its appearance was as crystal, and the wheels thereof as the shining sun, and there was the vision of cherubim.

11 And from underneath the throne came streams of flaming fire so that I could not look thereon.

12 And the Great Glory sat thereon, and his raiment shone more brightly than the sun and was whiter than any snow.

13 None of the angels could enter and behold his face by reason of the magnificence and glory, and no flesh could behold him.

14 The flaming fire was around him, and a great fire stood before him, and none around could draw nigh him — ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him, yet he needed no counselor.

15 And the most holy ones who were nigh to him did not leave by night nor depart from him.

16 And until then I had been prostrate on my face, trembling, and YHWH called me with his own mouth, and said to me: Come hither, Chanokh, and hear my word.

17 And one of the holy ones came to me and waked me, and he made me rise up and approach the door, and I bowed my face downwards.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnc (4Q204) and 4QEnd (4Q205) preserve substantial portions of Chapter 14 in Aramaic. The throne vision of Chapter 14 is one of the most significant texts in the Qumran corpus — it is the oldest surviving example of merkavah — divine chariot-throne — mysticism in the Hebrew tradition, predating the extensive later Rabbinic Heikhalot literature by centuries.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 14 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14:19-20 — the Great Glory sat on the throne, and his raiment shone more brightly than the sun and was whiter than any snow. None of the angels could enter and behold his face. Chanokh's throne vision is the foundational text of the divine chariot mysticism tradition. The imagery — crystal walls, fiery cherubim, streaming fire beneath the throne, ten thousand times ten thousand attendants — is the background against which Yehezkel's chariot vision (Yehezkel 1) and Daniyel's Ancient of Days vision (Daniyel 7) are to be read. The Qumran Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice (4QShirShab) elaborate the same heavenly temple imagery. The Book of the Watchers is not peripheral literature — it is the source text for a continuous tradition of heavenly throne mysticism running through the entire prophetic corpus.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 15 YHWH's Answer to Chanokh — The Origin of the Evil Spirits

1 And he answered and said to me, and I heard his voice: Fear not, Chanokh, you righteous man and scribe of righteousness — approach hither and hear my voice.

2 And go, say to the Watchers of heaven, who have sent you to intercede for them: You should intercede for men, and not men for you.

3 Why have you left the high, holy, and eternal heaven, and lain with women, and defiled yourselves with the daughters of men and taken to yourselves wives, and done like the children of earth, and begotten giants as your sons?

4 And though you were holy, spiritual, living the eternal life, you have defiled yourselves with the blood of women, and have begotten children with the blood of flesh, and, as the children of men, have lusted after flesh and blood as those also do who die and perish.

5 Therefore have I given them wives also that they might impregnate them, and beget children by them, that thus nothing might be wanting to them on earth.

6 But you were formerly spiritual, living the eternal life, and immortal for all generations of the world.

7 And therefore I have not appointed wives for you — for as for the spiritual ones of heaven, in heaven is their dwelling.

8 And now, the giants, who are produced from the spirits and flesh, shall be called evil spirits upon the earth, and on the earth shall be their dwelling.

9 Evil spirits have proceeded from their bodies — because they are born from men and from the holy Watchers is their beginning and primal origin. They shall be evil spirits on earth, and evil spirits shall they be called.

10 As for the spirits of heaven, in heaven shall be their dwelling — but as for the spirits of the earth which were born upon the earth, on the earth shall be their dwelling.

11 And the spirits of the giants afflict, oppress, destroy, attack, do battle, and work destruction on the earth, and cause trouble. They take no food, but nevertheless hunger and thirst and cause offences.

12 And these spirits shall rise up against the children of men and against the women, because they have proceeded from them.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnd (4Q205) preserves portions of Chapter 15 in Aramaic. The theological explanation of the origin of evil spirits in verses 15:8-12 is one of the most doctrinally significant passages in the DSS Chanokh corpus.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 15 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 15:8-12 — the giants, produced from spirits and flesh, when they die their bodies perish but their spirits remain on earth as evil spirits. This is the Book of the Watchers' explanation for the origin of demonic beings. They are not fallen angels — the fallen Watchers themselves are bound underground, imprisoned until judgment. The evil spirits that roam the earth are the disembodied spirits of the dead Nephilim — beings of mixed nature, neither fully heavenly nor fully earthly, with no proper dwelling. They hunger but cannot eat, thirst but cannot drink — and therefore attack, oppress, and destroy the living. This cosmology is the background for all Second Temple period demonology, including the material found in the War Scroll's treatment of the prince of darkness and his forces.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 16 The Final Rejection of the Watchers' Petition

1 From the days of the slaughter and destruction and death of the giants, from the souls of whose flesh the spirits, having gone forth, shall destroy without incurring judgment — thus shall they destroy until the day of the consummation, the great judgment in which the age shall be consummated, over the Watchers and the godless, and shall be wholly consummated.

2 And now as to the Watchers who have sent you to intercede for them, who had been aforetime in heaven, say to them:

3 You have been in heaven, but all the mysteries had not yet been revealed to you, and you knew worthless ones, and these in the hardness of your hearts you have made known to the women, and through these mysteries women and men work much evil on earth.

4 Say to them therefore: You have no peace.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnd (4Q205) preserves portions of Chapter 16 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 16 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 16:4 — say to them: you have no peace. YHWH's final word to the Watchers through Chanokh is the denial of shalom — the covenant word of wholeness, completeness, and right relationship. Shalom is impossible for those who have permanently broken the created order and transmitted forbidden knowledge to corrupt humanity. The denial of peace to the wicked — first stated in Chanokh — becomes a recurring prophetic declaration. Yesha'yahu 48:22 and 57:21 repeat it: there is no peace for the wicked. The phrase originates here in the Book of the Watchers as YHWH's direct word to the imprisoned Watchers through his scribe Chanokh.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 17 The First Journey — The Rivers of Fire — The Foundations of the Earth

1 And they took and brought me to a place in which those who were there were like flaming fire, and, when they wished, they appeared as men.

2 And they brought me to the place of darkness, and to a mountain the point of whose summit reached to heaven.

3 And I saw the places of the luminaries and the treasuries of the stars and of the thunder and in the uttermost depths, where were a fiery bow and arrows and their quiver, and a fiery sword and all the lightnings.

4 And they took me to the living waters, and to the fire of the west, which receives every setting of the sun.

5 And I came to a river of fire in which the fire flows like water and discharges itself into the great sea towards the west.

6 I saw the great rivers and came to the great river and to the great darkness, and went to the place where no flesh walks.

7 I saw the mountains of the darkness of winter and the place from where all the waters of the deep flow.

8 I saw the mouths of all the rivers of the earth and the mouth of the deep.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEne (4Q206) preserves portions of Chapter 17 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 17 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Chapters 17-19 and 21-36 record Chanokh's guided tours of the cosmic extremities — the foundations of the earth, the places of judgment for the Watchers and the dead, the holy mountain, the ends of the earth. The geography of the journeys reflects the ancient Afro-Asiatic cosmological understanding: a flat or domed earth with specific locations for the storehouses of weather, the rivers of fire, the mountains at the edges of creation, and the places of punishment and reward. The journeys function as cosmological confirmation of the theological claims made in Chapters 6-16.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 18 The Ends of the Earth — The Pit of the Stars — The Prison of the Watchers

1 I saw also another place having gatherings of waters.

2 And therein I saw a great and high mountain and hard rock, and four beautiful places and they were deep, wide, and very smooth. How smooth is the earth and how deep and dark it is to see.

3 Then Rafa'el answered, one of the holy angels who was with me, and said unto me: These beautiful places are intended as a gathering place for the stars and the hosts of heaven.

4 And they shall be assembled here, and this shall be their dwelling place, and this is the holy place where they shall remain.

5 And I saw how the winds carry the feeling of the vault of the heaven, and how — having flown with their wing — they cause the circumference of the sun to rotate.

6 And I saw at the end of the earth the firmament of the heaven above. And I proceeded and saw a place which burns day and night, where there are seven mountains of magnificent stones, three towards the east, and three towards the south.

7 And as for those towards the east, one was of coloured stone, and one of pearl, and one of jacinth, and those towards the south of red stone.

8 And beyond these mountains I saw another region to the east of the ends of the earth, where I saw a deep abyss, with columns of heavenly fire, and among them I saw columns of fire fall, which were beyond measure alike towards the height and towards the depth.

9 And beyond that abyss I saw a place which had no firmament of the heaven above, and no firmly founded earth beneath it — there was no water upon it, and no birds, but it was a waste and horrible place.

10 I saw there seven stars like great burning mountains, and to me, when I inquired regarding them, the angel said: This place is the end of heaven and earth — this has become a prison for the stars and the host of heaven.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEne (4Q206) preserves portions of Chapter 18 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 18 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18:16 — this place is the prison for the stars and the host of heaven. The seven imprisoned stars are a category distinct from the Watchers — these are stars that have abandoned their assigned orbits. The ancient cosmology of Chanokh encompasses the judgment of heavenly luminaries alongside the judgment of the fallen Watchers. Disobedience of the created order at the astronomical level — stars leaving their courses — receives the same judgment as the Watchers who left their heavenly stations. Creation's faithfulness to its ordained order is the absolute standard in the Book of the Watchers.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 19 Uriel Explains the Watchers' Place of Punishment

1 And Uriel said to me: Here shall stand the angels who have connected themselves with women, and their spirits assuming many different forms are defiling mankind and shall lead them astray into sacrificing to demons as deities — here shall they stand, till the day of the great judgment in which they shall be judged till they are made an end of.

2 And the women also of the angels who went astray shall become sirens.

3 And I, Chanokh, alone saw the vision, the ends of all things, and no man shall see as I have seen.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEne (4Q206) preserves a portion of Chapter 19.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 19 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 19:3 — I, Chanokh, alone saw the vision, the ends of all things, and no man shall see as I have seen. The Book of the Watchers closes its first journey section with Chanokh's first-person declaration of unique visionary standing. He has been taken to the cosmic extremities, shown the prisons of the fallen heavenly beings, and seen the ends of all things. The claim is total and exclusive. This is consistent with Bereshit 5:24 — Chanokh walked with The Creator and was not, for The Creator took him. His unique translation — not dying but being taken — is the basis for his unique visionary access.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 20 The Names and Functions of the Seven Holy Angels

1 And these are the names of the holy angels who watch.

2 Uriel, one of the holy angels, who is over the world and over Tartarus.

3 Rafa'el, one of the holy angels, who is over the spirits of men.

4 Ragu'el, one of the holy angels who takes vengeance on the world of the luminaries.

5 Mikha'el, one of the holy angels, to wit, he that is set over the best part of mankind and over chaos.

6 Saraqael, one of the holy angels, who is set over the spirits, who sin in the spirit.

7 Gavri'el, one of the holy angels, who is over Paradise and the serpents and the Cherubim.

8 Remi'el, one of the holy angels, whom The Creator set over those who rise.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEne (4Q206) preserves portions of Chapter 20 in Aramaic. The list of seven holy angels with their domains is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 20 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The seven holy angels of Chapter 20 are the counter-structure to the fallen Watchers. Where the Watchers abandoned their assigned cosmic functions and defiled themselves with humanity, the seven holy angels remain in their stations, each governing a specific domain of the created order. Mikha'el, Rafa'el, Gavri'el, and Uriel appear in Chapter 9 as the four who brought the report of earth's corruption to YHWH. Chapter 20 expands to seven. The Qumran Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice and the War Scroll both draw on the same angelological framework.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 21 The Place of the Imprisoned Stars — The Abyss of the Fallen Watchers

1 And I proceeded to where things were chaotic.

2 And I saw there something horrible — I saw neither a heaven above nor a firmly founded earth, but a place chaotic and horrible.

3 And there I saw seven stars of the heaven bound together in it, like great mountains and burning with fire.

4 Then I said: For what sin are they bound, and on what account have they been cast in hither?

5 Then said Uriel, one of the holy angels, who was with me, and was chief over them, and said: Chanokh, why do you ask, and why are you eager for the truth?

6 These are of the number of the stars of heaven, which have transgressed the commandment of YHWH, and are bound here till ten thousand years, the time entailed by their sins, are consummated.

7 And from thence I went to another place, which was still more horrible than the former, and I saw a horrible thing — a great fire there which burnt and blazed, and the place was cleft as far as the abyss, being full of great descending columns of fire.

8 Neither its extent or magnitude could I see, nor could I conjecture.

9 Then I said: How fearful is the place and how terrible to look upon.

10 Then Uriel answered me, one of the holy angels who was with me, and said unto me: Chanokh, why have you such fear and affright?

11 And I answered: Because of this fearful place, and because of the spectacle of the pain.

12 And he said unto me: This place is the prison of the angels, and here they will be imprisoned forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnf (4Q207) preserves portions of Chapter 21 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 21 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 21:12 — this place is the prison of the angels, and here they will be imprisoned forever. The abyss of Chapter 21 is the permanent prison of the fallen Watchers — distinct from their temporary binding described in Chapter 10. The ten-thousand-year sentence of verse 21:6 for the transgressing stars contrasts with the forever imprisonment of the Watchers. The geography of punishment in Chanokh is layered and specific — different categories of fallen beings have different prisons with different sentences. The Book of the Watchers operates with a detailed and internally consistent eschatological geography.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 22 The Mountain of the Dead — The Four Hollow Places — The Spirits Await Judgment

1 And thence I went to another place, and he showed me in the west another great and high mountain and of hard rock.

2 And there were four hollow places in it, deep and wide and very smooth. Three of them were dark and one bright, and there was a fountain of water in its midst.

3 And I said: How smooth are the hollow places and how deep and dark to view.

4 Then Rafa'el answered, one of the holy angels who was with me, and said unto me: These hollow places have been created for this very purpose, that the spirits of the souls of the dead should assemble therein — yea, that all the souls of the children of men should assemble here.

5 And these places have been made to receive them till the day of their judgment and till their appointed period, till the great judgment comes upon them.

6 Then I saw the spirit of a dead man, and his voice went forth to heaven and made suit.

7 And I asked Rafa'el the angel who was with me, and I said unto him: This spirit — whose is it whose voice goes forth and makes suit to heaven?

8 And he answered me saying: This is the spirit of Hevel, who was slain by Qa'in his brother, and he makes his suit against him till his seed is destroyed from the face of the earth, and his seed is annihilated from among the seed of men.

9 Then I asked regarding it, and regarding all the hollow places: Why is one separated from the other?

10 And he answered me and said unto me: These three have been made that the spirits of the dead might be separated. And such a division has been made for the spirits of the righteous, in which there is the bright spring of water.

11 And such has been made for sinners when they die and are buried in the earth and judgment has not been executed on them during their lifetime.

12 Here their spirits shall be set apart in this great pain till the great day of judgment and punishment and torment of those who curse forever and retribution for their spirits.

13 There he shall bind them forever. And such a division has been made for the spirits of those who make their suit, who make disclosures concerning their destruction, when they were slain in the days of the sinners.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnf (4Q207) and 4QEng (4Q212) preserve portions of Chapter 22 in Aramaic. The description of the four compartments for the dead is one of the most extensively discussed passages in the DSS Chanokh literature.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 22 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 22:9 — this is the spirit of Hevel, who was slain by Qa'in, and he makes his suit against him till his seed is destroyed. The first murder in human history — Qa'in and Hevel — has an eschatological continuation in Chanokh. Hevel's spirit continues to petition against Qa'in from the holding place of the righteous dead. The blood that cried from the ground in Bereshit 4:10 has a spiritual correspondent — Hevel's spirit in the hollow place of the righteous, still crying for justice. The ancient Hebrew understanding of justice requires that every unresolved wrong be resolved at the final judgment. No cry goes unanswered. The cry is simply held in the cosmic record until the day of judgment.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 23 The Fire That Never Rests

1 From thence I went to another place to the west of the ends of the earth.

2 And I saw a burning fire which ran without resting, and paused not from its course day or night but ran regularly.

3 And I asked saying: What is this which rests not?

4 Then Ragu'el, one of the holy angels who was with me, answered me and said unto me: This course of fire which you have seen is the fire in the west which persecutes all the luminaries of heaven.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves a portion of Chapter 23.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 23 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The fire that never rests in the west — the place where the sun sets — is part of the astronomical cosmology of Chanokh that reaches its full treatment in the Astronomical Book of Chapters 72-82. The fire pursues the luminaries as they complete their circuits. The created order of light and darkness is maintained by active cosmic forces, not by passive mechanical motion.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 24 The Seven Mountains — The Tree of Life

1 From thence I went to another place of the earth, and he showed me a mountain range of fire which burnt day and night.

2 And I went beyond it and saw seven magnificent mountains all differing each from the other, and the stones thereof were magnificent and beautiful, magnificent as a whole, of glorious appearance and fair exterior.

3 Three mountains were towards the east, one on the other, and three towards the south, one on the other, and deep rough ravines, no one of which joined with any other.

4 And the seventh mountain was in the midst of these, and it excelled them in height, resembling the seat of a throne, and fragrant trees encircled the throne.

5 And among them was a tree such as I had never yet smelt, neither was any among them nor were others like it — it had a fragrance beyond all fragrance, and its leaves and blooms and wood wither not forever, and its fruit is beautiful, and its fruit resembles the dates of a palm.

6 Then I said: How beautiful is this tree, and how attractive is its look.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 24 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 24 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The fragrant tree of Chapter 24 — whose leaves and blooms and wood never wither, whose fragrance exceeds all fragrance — is identified in Chapter 25 as the Tree of Life that will be given to the righteous at the great judgment. Its location in the seventh mountain at the center of the mountain range connects it to the sacred center geography of the ancient Hebrew cosmological imagination. The Mishkan is at the center of the camp. The holy of holies is at the center of the Mishkan. The Tree of Life is at the center of the seven mountains at the ends of the earth.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 25 Mikha'el Explains the Tree of Life — The Final Promise to the Righteous

1 And Mikha'el answered me, one of the holy angels who was with me, and was their chief, and said unto me:

2 Chanokh, why do you ask me regarding the fragrance of the tree, and why do you wish to learn the truth?

3 Then I answered him saying: I wish to know about everything, but especially about this tree.

4 And he answered saying: This high mountain which you have seen, whose summit is like the throne of The Creator, is his throne, where the Holy Great One, YHWH of Glory, the Eternal King, will sit, when he shall come down to visit the earth with goodness.

5 And as for this fragrant tree no mortal is permitted to touch it till the great judgment, when he shall take vengeance on all and bring everything to its consummation forever — it shall then be given to the righteous and holy.

6 Its fruit shall be for food to the elect — it shall be transplanted to the holy place, to the temple of YHWH, the Eternal King.

7 Then shall they rejoice with joy and be glad, and into the holy place shall they enter — and its fragrance shall be in their bones, and they shall live a long life on earth, such as your fathers lived, and in their days shall no sorrow or plague or torment or calamity touch them.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 25 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 25 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 25:5-6 — the Tree of Life will be given to the righteous at the great judgment, transplanted to the holy place, to the temple of YHWH. The Tree of Life placed in Gan Eden in Bereshit 2-3, from which humanity was excluded after the transgression of Chavah and Adam, is not destroyed or permanently inaccessible. In Chanokh's vision it stands at the ends of the earth in the cosmic mountains, guarded and waiting. At the great judgment it will be transplanted to the holy place — the temple of YHWH — and given to the righteous elect. The expulsion from the garden in Bereshit is reversed at the final judgment. Access to the Tree of Life is restored through the covenant's ultimate fulfillment.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 26 The Holy Mountain and the Accursed Valley

1 And from thence I went to the middle of the earth, and I saw a blessed place in which there were trees with branches abiding and blooming of a dismembered tree.

2 And there I saw a holy mountain, and underneath the mountain to the east there was a stream and it flowed towards the south.

3 And I saw towards the east another mountain higher than this, and between them a deep and narrow ravine — in it also ran a stream underneath the mountain.

4 And to the west thereof there was another mountain, lower than the former and of small elevation, and a ravine deep and dry between them, and another deep and dry ravine was at the extremities of the three mountains.

5 And all the ravines were deep and narrow, being formed of hard rock, and trees were not planted upon them.

6 And I marveled at the rocks, and I marveled at the ravine — yea, I marveled very much.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 26 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 26 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The holy mountain at the middle of the earth in Chapter 26 is understood in the ancient tradition as the location of Yerushalayim and the Temple Mount — the omphalos, the navel of the earth. The accursed valley to the west of the holy mountain — where the dead suffer — corresponds to the Valley of Hinnom — Ge-Hinnom — west of Yerushalayim, which became the location of Molekh worship and later the metaphor for the place of punishment for the wicked. The geography of Chanokh's vision maps onto the physical geography of the covenant land.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 27 The Accursed Valley — The Judgment of the Wicked

1 Then Uriel answered me, one of the holy angels who was with me, and said unto me: This accursed valley is for those who are accursed forever — here shall all the accursed be gathered together who utter with their lips against YHWH unseemly words and of his glory speak hard things.

2 Here shall they be gathered together, and here shall be their place of judgment.

3 In the last days there shall be upon them the spectacle of righteous judgment in the presence of the righteous forever — those who have been merciful shall bless YHWH of glory, the Eternal King.

4 In the days of judgment over the former, they shall bless him for the mercy in accordance with which he has assigned them their lot.

5 Then I blessed YHWH of Glory and set forth his glory and lauded him gloriously.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 27 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 27 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The accursed valley of Chapter 27 — where those who speak blasphemy against YHWH are gathered for judgment — is the developed form of the Ge-Hinnom concept. Those who utter unseemly words against YHWH's glory are specifically identified as the population of this place. The emphasis on speech — unseemly words, hard things — connects to the Torah's treatment of the name of YHWH and the prohibition against taking it in vain. Chanokh's judgment geography makes blasphemy a category with a specific cosmic destination.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 28 The Journey to the East — Spice Trees and Cinnamon

1 And from thence I went towards the east, into the midst of the mountain range of the desert, and I saw a wilderness, and it was solitary, full of trees and plants.

2 And water gushed forth from above.

3 Rushing like a copious watercourse towards the north-west it caused clouds and dew to ascend on every side.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves a portion of Chapter 28.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 28 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The eastern journeys of Chapters 28-32 move through the paradise regions of the earth — the spice-bearing lands, the garden regions, the paradisiacal trees. The ancient geography places the origins of aromatic spices — cinnamon, pepper, ginger — in the eastern and southern regions of the earth, consistent with the ancient trade routes through the Afro-Asiatic corridor connecting the Nile Valley with the Arabian peninsula and the subcontinent.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 29 The Fragrant Trees and the Living Waters of the East

1 And beyond these, I went afar to the east, and I saw another place, a valley full of water.

2 And therein there was a tree, the colour of fragrant trees such as the mastic.

3 And on the sides of those valleys I saw fragrant cinnamon. And beyond these I proceeded to the east.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves a portion of Chapter 29.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 29 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The specific identification of cinnamon and mastic in the eastern paradise regions reflects the actual geography of ancient spice trade. Cinnamon was known in the ancient world as originating from the regions of northeast Africa and Arabia — consistent with the Afro-Asiatic geographic frame of the Chanokh literature. The ancient Hebrew world's knowledge of distant geography is encoded in the cosmic journey narratives of Chanokh.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 30 The Trees of Good Repute — Pepper and Incense

1 And I saw other mountains, and among them were groves of trees, and there flowed out from them nectar, which is named sarara and galbanum.

2 And beyond these mountains I saw another mountain to the east of the ends of the earth, on which were aloe-trees and all the trees were full of stacte, being like almond-trees.

3 And when one burnt it, it smelt sweeter than any fragrant odour.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves a portion of Chapter 30.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 30 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Galbanum, stacte, and aloe are among the specific aromatic substances named in the Mishkan incense formula of Shemot 30:34. The Chanokh journey through the paradise regions of the earth is simultaneously a journey through the source regions of the sacred incense components used in the Mishkan. The holiness of the sanctuary's fragrance is connected to the paradise geography at the ends of the earth.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 31 Towards the North-East — Incense Mountains

1 And beyond these fragrant trees I looked towards the north-east over the mountains, and I saw seven mountains full of choice nard and fragrant trees and cinnamon and pepper.

2 And from thence I went over the summits of all these mountains, far towards the east of the earth, and passed above the Erythraean sea and went far from it, and passed over the angel Zotiel.

3 And I came to the Garden of Righteousness, and saw beyond those trees many large trees growing there and of goodly fragrance, large, very beautiful and glorious, and the tree of wisdom whereof they eat and know great wisdom.

4 That tree is in height like the fir, and its leaves are like those of the Carob tree, and its fruit is like the clusters of the vine, very beautiful, and the fragrance of the tree penetrates afar.

5 Then I said: How beautiful is the tree, and how attractive is its look.

6 Then Rafa'el, the holy angel who was with me, answered me and said: This is the tree of wisdom, of which your father old and your aged mother, who were before you, have eaten, and they learnt wisdom and their eyes were opened, and they knew that they were naked and they were driven out of the garden.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 31 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 31 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 31:3-6 — the Garden of Righteousness contains the tree of wisdom — the tree from which Adam and Chavah ate in Bereshit 3. Chanokh reaches the Garden of Eden in the course of his cosmic journey. Rafa'el identifies the tree and names the first parents — your father old and your aged mother. The garden still exists in Chanokh's cosmology — it was not destroyed at the expulsion. It remains at the ends of the earth, containing both the tree of wisdom and, in a separate mountain, the Tree of Life. The expulsion from the garden in Bereshit is a relocation from the cosmic center, not the destruction of paradise.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 32 The Gate of Heaven — The Ends of the Earth

1 After these fragrant odours, as I looked towards the north over the mountains I saw seven mountains full of choice nard and mastic and cinnamon and pepper.

2 And thence I went over the summits of those mountains, far towards the east, and passed over the Erythraean sea, and went far from it, and passed over the angel Zotiel.

3 And I came to the Garden of Righteousness, I and from afar off trees more numerous than these trees and great — two trees there, very great, very magnificent, and glorious, and magnificent, and the tree of knowledge, whose holy fruit they eat and know great wisdom.

4 And it has a height like the fir tree, and its leaves are like those of the carob tree, and its fruit is like the clusters of the vine, very beautiful, and the fragrance of the tree penetrates afar off.

5 And I said: How beautiful is the tree, and how attractive is its look.

6 And the holy angel Rafa'el who was with me, answered and said: This is the tree of wisdom, of which your old father and aged mother ate, who were before you, and they learn wisdom and their eyes were opened, and they knew that they were naked — they were driven out of the garden.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 32 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 32 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The repetition in Chapters 31-32 reflects the manuscript tradition of the Book of the Watchers — some passages appear in slightly different forms in the different textual witnesses. Both the DSS Aramaic and the Ge'ez preserve the variant forms. The encounter with the tree of wisdom and the identification of Adam and Chavah as the first parents who ate from it is preserved consistently across both textual traditions.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 33 The Northern Extremities — The Stars and Their Stations

1 And from thence I went to the ends of the earth and saw there great beasts, and each differed from the other — and birds also differing in appearance and beauty and voice, the one differing from the other.

2 And to the east of those beasts I saw the ends of the earth whereon the heaven rests, and the portals of the heaven open.

3 And I saw how the stars of heaven come forth, and I counted the portals out of which they proceed, and wrote down all their outlets, of each individual star by itself, according to their number and their names, their courses and their positions, and their times and their months, as Uriel the holy angel who was with me showed me.

4 He showed all things to me and wrote them down for me — also their names he wrote for me, and their laws and their companies.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 33 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 33 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 33:3-4 — Chanokh counts the portals of the stars and writes down their outlets, names, courses, and times as Uriel shows him. This is the beginning of the astronomical curriculum that receives its full treatment in the Astronomical Book — Chapters 72-82. Chanokh's role as a scribe is central to the entire book: he writes what he sees and is shown. The knowledge of the heavenly calendar — preserved in written form by Chanokh — is then transmitted to his son Metushelakh in Chapter 82. The covenant calendar, the appointed times of YHWH, is grounded in the astronomical order that Chanokh records.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 34 The Northern Portals of the Wind

1 And from thence I went towards the north to the ends of the earth, and there I saw a great and glorious device at the ends of the whole earth.

2 And here I saw three portals of heaven open in the heaven — through each of them proceed north winds, and when they blow there is cold, hail, frost, snow, dew, and rain.

3 And out of one portal they blow for good, but when they blow through the other two portals, it is with violence and affliction on the earth, and they blow with violence.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves a portion of Chapter 34.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 34 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The three northern portals of Chapter 34 are part of the meteorological cosmology of Chanokh — the storehouses of wind, snow, hail, frost, and rain that YHWH controls and deploys. The same imagery appears in Iyov 38:22 — have you entered the storehouses of the snow, and have you seen the storehouses of the hail? The meteorological storehouses are not mythological decoration — they are the cosmological infrastructure of the covenant land's dependence on YHWH's provision of rain and weather described in Devarim 11:12-14.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 35 The Western Portals

1 And after that I saw the other portals — the portals of the west — three portals: out of one of these blows dew and rain, and from the other two blows hail and frost.

2 Out of the first portal blows dew and rain, and the second portal blows hail and frost, and from the third blows snow and cold.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves a portion of Chapter 35.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 35 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The western portals mirror the northern portals of Chapter 34. The weather system of the ancient cosmology is directional — winds from the north bring cold and snow; portals in the west release dew and rain alongside hail and frost. The Astronomical Book's treatment of seasonal variation is anticipated in these brief descriptions of the atmospheric portals.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 36 The Southern Portals — The Final Blessing — The End of the Book of the Watchers

1 And after that I saw the other portals — the portals of the south — and there I saw three portals through which there go forth the dew and rain and the hot wind.

2 And from the second portal there go forth fragrant smells, and dew and rain, and from the third portal there goes forth wind and dust.

3 And after these come the portals of the east, and out of them there go forth the dew and rain and fine air, and also hot winds blowing — through the third portal there go forth winds and dust.

4 And now I have shown you, Chanokh, all the portals, and all the winds and all the phenomena.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves a portion of Chapter 36 in Aramaic. Chapter 36 closes the Book of the Watchers — the first and most extensively DSS-attested section of the Book of Chanokh. The six DSS manuscripts 4QEna through 4QEng together provide fragmentary coverage of most of the Book of the Watchers, confirming that this section was intensively studied at Qumran.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 36 in full, completing the Book of the Watchers section.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 36:4 — I have shown you, Chanokh, all the portals, and all the winds and all the phenomena. Uriel's declaration closes the cosmic journey of the Book of the Watchers. Chanokh has been shown the complete structure of the physical creation — the winds, the stars, the portals of weather, the prisons of the fallen angels, the holding places of the dead, the Tree of Life, the Garden of Righteousness, the holy mountain, the accursed valley. The Book of the Watchers is a complete cosmological curriculum. The scribe of righteousness has been given a total education in the architecture of creation, judgment, and restoration. Everything he has seen will be transmitted — to Metushelakh in Chapters 82-83, and through the book itself to every generation of the covenant people who read it.

END OF PART ONE — THE BOOK OF THE WATCHERS
